1: To loipon proseuchesthe adelphoi peri hēmōn hina ho logos tou kyriou trechē kai doxazētai kathōs kai pros hymas: 'Finally, brothers, pray for us, that the word of the Lord may speed ahead (trechē) and be honored (doxazētai), as happened among you.' Trechō (to run swiftly, to progress rapidly) echoes Psalms 147:15 (his word runs swiftly). Doxazētai (to be honored, to be glorified) — the gospel's reception is described as the glorification of the Lord's word. The Thessalonians' own reception of the gospel is the model and precedent Paul cites.
2: kai hina rhysthōmen apo tōn atopōn kai ponērōn anthrōpōn ou gar pantōn hē pistis: 'and that we may be delivered from wicked and evil people. For not all have faith.' Atopōn (out-of-place, improper, absurd — used for those who are out of their proper moral category) and ponērōn (evil, actively harmful). The non-universality of faith (ou gar pantōn hē pistis — for faith is not of all) is a striking understatement: most people Paul encountered opposed the gospel rather than receiving it.
3: Pistos de estin ho kyrios hos stērixei hymas kai phylaxei apo tou ponērou: 'But the Lord is faithful (pistos). He will establish you and guard you against the evil one (tou ponērou).' The contrast is sharp: not all humans are faithful (2 Thessalonians 3:2), but the Lord is. Pistos (faithful, trustworthy — from pistis) applied to the kyrios: the divine faithfulness is the ground of the congregation's stability despite human unfaithfulness. Tou ponērou — either the evil one (Satan, as in Matthew 6:13) or evil in general. The parallel with the Lord's Prayer (deliver us from the evil one) is suggestive.
4: Pepoithamen de en kyriō eph' hymas hoti ha parangellomen [kai] poieite kai poiēsete: 'And we have confidence in the Lord about you, that you are doing and will do the things that we command.' The apostolic confidence is qualified en kyriō (in the Lord) — not confidence in the Thessalonians' natural reliability but in the Lord's faithfulness working through them. Parangellomen (we command, we charge authoritatively) — a stronger word than parakalō (I appeal/encourage), reflecting military command language.
5: Ho de kyrios kateuthynai hymōn tas kardias eis tēn agapēn tou theou kai eis tēn hypomonēn tou Christou: 'May the Lord direct your hearts to the love of God and to the steadfastness of Christ (hypomonēn tou Christou).' Kateuthynai (to direct straight, to guide) — the wish-prayer form. Hypomonē tou Christou (the endurance/steadfastness of Christ or endurance for/like Christ) — either the patient endurance that belongs to Christ (subjective genitive: Christ's own hypomonē modeled in Hebrews 12:2) or the endurance directed toward Christ's coming. The ambiguity is productive: both readings are theologically valid.
6: Parangellomen de hymin adelphoi en onomati tou kyriou Iēsou Christou stellesthai hymas apo pantos adelphou ataktōs peripatountos kai mē kata tēn paradosin hēn parelabosan par' hēmōn: 'Now we command you, brothers, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, to keep away (stellesthai) from any brother who is walking in idleness (ataktōs) and not in accord with the tradition they received from us.' Ataktōs (in a disorderly manner, out of rank) — from ataktos, the military term for a soldier who breaks formation. The ataktoi are not merely lazy but have abandoned their ordered place in the community. Stellesthai (to keep away from, to avoid, to lower sail of — a nautical term) — the shunning is partial, not total excommunication (2 Thessalonians 3:15: treat as a brother).
7: Autoi gar oidate pōs dei mimeisthai hēmas hoti ouk ētaktēsamen en hymin: 'For you yourselves know how you ought to imitate us (mimeisthai), because we were not idle (ētaktēsamen) when we were with you.' The mimesis (imitation) appeal: the apostolic team modeled the ordered life they are now commanding. Ētaktēsamen (we were not disorderly/idle) — the same ataktos root negated: Paul explicitly says he did not practice the idleness he is now condemning.
8: oude dōrean arton ephagomen para tinos alla en kopō kai mochthō nykta kai hēmeran ergazomenoi pros to mē epibarēsai tina hymōn: 'nor did we eat anyone's bread without paying for it, but with toil (kopō) and labor (mochthō) we worked night and day, that we might not be a burden to any of you.' Kopō kai mochthō (toil and labor) — the double noun intensifies the effort. Nykta kai hēmeran (night and day) — working beyond normal hours. Paul's manual labor during his mission (1 Corinthians 9:6: Barnabas and I who work for our living; 1 Thessalonians 2:9: working night and day) was a deliberate choice to avoid financial dependency that could compromise his message or his congregations.
9: ouch hoti ouk echomen exousian alla hina heautous typon dōmen hymin eis to mimeisthai hēmas: 'It was not because we do not have that right (exousian), but to give you in ourselves an example (typon) to imitate.' Exousia (authority, right) — Paul had the apostolic right to financial support from the congregations (1 Corinthians 9:3-14: do we not have the right to eat and drink? The Lord commanded that those who proclaim the gospel should get their living by the gospel). He waived it to set a pattern (typos) — a visible model to be replicated.
10: kai gar hote ēmen pros hymas touto parēngelomen hymin hoti ei tis ou thelei ergazesthai mēde esthiō: 'For even when we were with you, we would give you this command (parēngelomen): If anyone is not willing to work, let him not eat.' The si quis non vult operari nec manducet (if anyone is not willing to work, neither let him eat) was Paul's prior oral teaching, now restated in writing. The dominical echo is debated: some trace it to Jesus tradition (a logion not preserved in the Gospels); others read it as a creation-order principle (Genesis 3:19). The categorical form (mēde esthiō — neither let him eat) matches the categorical form of rabbinic rules.
11: akouomen gar tinas peripatountas en hymin ataktōs mēden ergazomenous alla periergazomenous: 'For we hear that some among you walk in idleness, not busy at work, but busybodies (periergazomenous).' The wordplay: mēden ergazomenous alla periergazomenous (doing no work but doing over-work in others' affairs — literally working around rather than working). Periergazomenos (a busybody, one who meddles in others' business) — the word appears only here in the NT. The connection between idleness and meddlesomeness: those not occupied with their own work fill the vacuum with interference in others' affairs.
12: tois de toioutois parangellomen kai parakaloumen en kyriō Iēsou Christō hina meta hēsychias ergazomenoi ton heautōn arton esthiōsin: 'Now such persons we command (parangellomen) and encourage (parakaloumen) in the Lord Jesus Christ to do their work quietly (meta hēsychias) and to eat their own bread.' Meta hēsychias (with quietness, in tranquility) — the opposite of the restless meddlesomeness of 2 Thessalonians 3:11. The command to eat ton heautōn arton (their own bread) contrasts with eating others' bread without payment (2 Thessalonians 3:8): the resolution is self-sustaining labor.
13: Hymeis de adelphoi mē enkakēsēte kalopoiountes: 'As for you, brothers, do not grow weary in doing good (kalopoiountes).' Enkakeō (to grow weary, to lose heart, to be cowardly in the face of difficulty) — the exhortation to the non-idle majority: the problem of the ataktoi must not cause the faithful majority to tire of doing good. Kalopoiountes (doing what is good/beautiful) is the broad category encompassing the generosity, work, and community care that the ataktoi were disrupting.
14: Ei de tis ouch hypakouei tō logō hēmōn dia tēs epistolēs touton sēmeiousthe mē synanamignysthai autō hina entrapē: 'If anyone does not obey what we say in this letter, take note of that person (sēmeiousthe), and have nothing to do with him (mē synanamignysthai), that he may be ashamed (entrapē).' Sēmeiousthe (mark, note down, single out) — the act of communal identification for purposes of discipline. Synanamignysthai (to mix with, to associate with) — social distancing from the unrepentant member, as in 1 Corinthians 5:9, 11. The goal (hina entrapē): that he may feel shame — social shame as a redemptive mechanism in an honor-shame culture.
15: kai mē hōs echthron hēgeisthe alla noutheteite hōs adelphon: 'Do not regard him as an enemy, but warn him as a brother.' The limit of the discipline: the shunned person remains a brother, not an enemy. Nouthetein (to warn, admonish, counsel — from nous + tithēmi: to put in mind) — the goal is restoration through frank admonition, not adversarial confrontation. This is the pastoral corrective to any punitive excess.
16: Autos de ho kyrios tēs eirēnēs dōē hymin tēn eirēnēn dia pantos en panti tropō. Ho kyrios meta pantōn hymōn: 'Now may the Lord of peace himself give you peace at all times in every way. The Lord be with you all.' Ho kyrios tēs eirēnēs (the Lord of peace) — peace as a divine attribute predicated of the Lord Jesus (Ephesians 2:14: he himself is our peace). Dia pantos en panti tropō (always in every manner/way) — the breadth of the benediction covers all circumstances. Ho kyrios meta pantōn hymōn (the Lord be with all of you) — the standard benediction of divine presence, echoing the OT Immanuel-promise (Isaiah 7:14; 2 Thessalonians 8:8-10).
17: Ho aspasmos tē emē cheiri Paulou ho estin sēmeion en pasē epistolē houtōs graphō: 'I, Paul, write this greeting with my own hand. This is the sign of genuineness in every letter of mine; it is the way I write.' Sēmeion (sign, mark of authentication) — the autograph as a distinguishing mark. The phrase en pasē epistolē (in every letter) implies Paul was known to authenticate his letters this way and that a letter lacking his hand-signature should be suspected. This directly responds to the concern about forged letters in 2 Thessalonians 2:2.
18: Hē charis tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou meta pantōn hymōn: 'The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all.' The final benediction matches 1 Thessalonians 5:28 almost exactly. The repeated pantōn (all) — even the ataktoi who have been disciplined are included in the grace that is wished for the whole congregation. The letter ends with a broader embrace than its internal disciplinary concerns might suggest.
